title: The Deal Starts Moving: Iran Oil Sanctions Lifted, Hormuz Reopens, Ukraine Strikes Crimea Logistics
subhead: A 60-day roadmap emerged from Switzerland, the US lifted Iranian oil sanctions, and Hormuz traffic is recovering — but the Pentagon wants $80 billion and Lebanon remains unresolved. Meanwhile, Ukraine dealt a significant blow to Russian logistics in Crimea.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks Yield a 60-Day Roadmap
sections.body:
US Vice President JD Vance and Iranian parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf concluded a first round of talks at the Burgenstock resort in Switzerland, with mediators Pakistan and Qatar announcing that the two sides agreed on a 'roadmap towards reaching a final deal within 60 days.' Technical working groups are expected to begin meeting this week on the MoU's implementation clauses.

Vance told reporters the talks laid 'a very good foundation for a successful final deal,' while acknowledging that 'the final deal is the house — we set the foundation, we haven't built the house.' The talks had rocky moments: Trump threatened on social media that the US would strike Iran 'harder' if Iran does not stop Hezbollah, prompting the Iranian delegation to temporarily withdraw in protest, according to Iranian media. An unspecified diplomat told Axios that the Iranian team had not left the venue and negotiations continued.

The roadmap sets a 60-day negotiating window — matching the MoU signed June 17 — to reach a final agreement covering Iran's nuclear programme, the status of the Strait of Hormuz, and a ceasefire on all fronts. Iran insists that clauses on Lebanon, economic relief, and Hormuz must be implemented before nuclear negotiations begin.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first formal negotiating timeline since the MoU was signed. The sequencing dispute — Iran wants economic and Lebanon clauses fulfilled before nuclear talks — is the core friction point that will determine whether the roadmap leads to a deal or collapses.

sections.heading: US Lifts Iranian Oil Sanctions, Hormuz Traffic Recovers
sections.body:
The US Treasury issued a 60-day general license on June 22 waiving sanctions on Iranian oil exports, authorising the production, delivery, and sale of Iranian crude, petrochemical, and petroleum products. The license lasts through August 21 and allows transactions in US dollars for oil purchases, freight, and insurance. It does not lift sanctions on Iranian sellers — only on the buyers.

According to Argus Media, the OFAC license allows unlimited sales of Iranian crude and even permits imports into the US for trans-shipment. Before the war, most Iranian crude went to Chinese independent refiners; with waivers in place, Chinese state-controlled companies and Indian buyers could re-enter the market. But many buyers remain cautious given the 60-day horizon and the risk of snapback sanctions if negotiations fail.

Iran's negotiator Qalibaf said Tehran agreed to establish a communication line for safe ship passage through the Strait of Hormuz. Shipping data tracked by Clarksons shows daily transits remain below the pre-war average of 125, but the trend is positive: 16 vessels transited between June 20-21, and four Qatari LNG tankers and two supertankers entered the Gulf on June 22. Trump declared Hormuz 'totally open.'

Brent crude fell over 3.5% to $77.70 per barrel on the news. Iran also confirmed the release of $12 billion in frozen assets, with Central Bank Governor Hemmati joining the Swiss delegation to work on releasing $6 billion blocked in Qatar.
sections.whyItMatters: The sanctions waiver is the most tangible implementation step taken since the MoU. It directly eases global oil prices and signals good faith, but the 60-day limit creates a countdown clock that could spook buyers and markets if progress stalls.

sections.heading: Lebanon Remains the Deal's Fault Line
sections.body:
The biggest unresolved tension is Lebanon. Iran is using the MoU's first clause — calling for a ceasefire on all fronts — to demand that the US compel Israel to halt operations against Hezbollah and withdraw from southern Lebanon. Israeli and Hezbollah attacks have largely paused since June 20, but Israeli forces continue clearing operations within the IDF's self-declared 'security zone,' which comprises hundreds of square miles of Lebanese territory.

Lebanese President Joseph Aoun spoke with senior US and Qatari officials about setting up a 'de-confliction cell' for Lebanon — a mechanism agreed to in the Swiss talks. Israel's National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir responded to the killing of four Israeli soldiers by Hezbollah on June 19 by calling for 'scorched earth in Lebanon,' stating 'all of Lebanon must burn.' Vance acknowledged the friction, saying that when 'a major explosion goes off in a civilian population centre in Beirut, and a lot of people who have nothing to do with Hezbollah lose their lives,' that is 'not acceptable.'

Israel was not included in the Swiss negotiations and has distanced itself from the US-Iran agreement, insisting it will not withdraw from southern Lebanon. The de-confliction cell is the only concrete mechanism so far to manage the Israel-Lebanon front, but its structure and enforcement remain undefined.
sections.whyItMatters: Lebanon is the clause that can blow up the entire deal. Iran has made a Hezbollah ceasefire its top priority; Israel refuses to withdraw. Without US pressure on Israel, Iran has a pretext to delay nuclear talks or revert to escalation.

sections.heading: Ukraine Strikes Kerch Strait, Crippling Russian Logistics in Crimea
sections.body:
Ukrainian forces launched a coordinated strike campaign overnight June 21 targeting Russian logistics across the Kerch Strait. According to ISW, Ukrainian SBU, USF, GUR, and SSO forces struck the Port of Kavkaz oil transshipment complex and the TES-Terminal-1 oil depot in occupied Kerch, setting both ablaze. Geolocated footage confirmed fires at both sites and at least three ferries burning at the Kerch Strait crossing. Ukrainian forces also disabled four S-400 radar stations and two Pantsir air defence systems on the Kerch Strait Bridge.

The effects were immediate. Crimea occupation governor Sergei Aksyonov ordered all gas stations to stop selling fuel to non-state entities. Sevastopol's occupation governor Mikhail Razvozhaev barred fuel sales entirely on June 22-23, shortened public transport hours, suspended ferry operations, and cancelled all outdoor events. Russia temporarily suspended Kerch Strait ferry service, diverting freight along the M-14 highway via occupied Mariupol, Melitopol, and Simferopol — a far longer route. Crimea's energy company Krymenergo introduced electricity consumption restrictions, with power outages reported across multiple cities.

The strikes are part of a systematic Ukrainian campaign to deny Russia both GLOCs into Crimea — the Kerch Strait crossing and the 'land bridge' through occupied southern Ukraine. Ukrainian forces have intensified strikes on Russian logistics in Kherson Oblast and Crimea throughout June, including railway bridges near Rozdolne and Chonhar.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kerch Strait strikes represent a significant Ukrainian operational success. With ferries damaged, the bridge restricted, and fuel rationed across Crimea, Russia's ability to sustain its occupation faces real strain. This is not a one-off — it's a sustained campaign to isolate Crimea logistically.

sections.heading: Pentagon Seeks $80 Billion for Iran War as Congress Balks
sections.body:
The Pentagon has told senators it needs roughly $80 billion, mostly to cover the cost of the US war against Iran, according to the Wall Street Journal and AP. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has been lobbying Capitol Hill, and Deputy Defense Secretary Stephen Feinberg notified congressional committees about the request last week. The $80 billion figure sits between the $29 billion in estimated war costs Hegseth cited in May and the $200 billion initially floated at the war's start — which included $11.3 billion for the first week alone.

The supplemental request faces an uncertain path. Senate Majority Leader John Thune said he expects a formal request and will 'work through it and see where the votes are,' but Democratic Senator Brian Schatz said he hasn't 'found anyone who wants to do this.' Senator Patty Murray told Hegseth: 'You're spending families' hard-earned tax dollars on a war that many strongly oppose.' Republicans may try to bundle the supplemental with disaster aid and agricultural relief to broaden support. Meanwhile, Trump's broader defence budget request — $1.5 trillion, a nearly 50% increase over current levels — adds to the political difficulty.
sections.whyItMatters: The $80 billion request signals that even if a deal holds, the war's cost is far from over. Munitions replenishment, equipment repair, and ongoing deployments don't stop with a ceasefire. The political fight over funding will be a preview of broader debates about the war's legitimacy and cost.

sections.heading: BRICS Security Summit in New Delhi as Iran War Reshapes Alignments
sections.body:
India hosted the BRICS National Security Advisers' meeting in New Delhi on June 22-23, with the Iran war and the Ukraine conflict topping the agenda. Indian National Security Advisor Ajit Doval held a bilateral meeting with Iranian official Ghadir Nezamipour, a senior member of Tehran's Supreme National Security Council, discussing the 'ongoing situation' in West Asia and India-Iran ties. China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi also attended, in what marks a slow improvement in India-China relations since the 2020 border clash.

The BRICS summit unfolded against a backdrop of shifting global alignments. India has long maintained cordial ties with Iran while balancing its relationship with the US — a position now under strain as the war reshapes Gulf security and energy flows. Iran's presence at the BRICS security meeting, even as a non-member, reflects the bloc's effort to position itself as a counterweight to Western-led diplomacy on the Iran file.

The NATO Defence Ministers' meeting on June 18 — the last before the Ankara Summit on July 7-8 — saw Secretary General Rutte push allies to commit to 5% of GDP on defence. Rutte stressed 'turning cash into combat-ready capabilities,' and the Nuclear Planning Group issued a statement reaffirming commitment to modernising NATO's nuclear deterrent. The Ukraine Defence Contact Group, attended by President Zelenskyy, confirmed further pledges of support through NATO's PURL initiative.
sections.whyItMatters: Two security architectures are taking shape simultaneously: BRICS offering a non-Western forum for crisis diplomacy, and NATO pressing toward 5% GDP spending and a 'NATO 3.0' reboot. The Iran war is accelerating both.

bottomLine: June 23 marks the first trading day since the US-Iran roadmap emerged from Switzerland — and the first with Iranian oil legally flowing again. The 60-day clock is ticking on negotiations that will determine whether the MoU becomes a lasting deal or another broken ceasefire. Lebanon remains the weakest link: Iran wants Israel out, Israel refuses to leave, and the de-confliction cell is the only mechanism bridging that gap. Meanwhile, Ukraine delivered a significant operational blow to Russian logistics in Crimea, and the Pentagon opened a $80 billion political fight in Washington that will test whether Congress will bankroll a war that a majority of the public has soured on.
